We were contacted by this firm regarding an alleged medical debt they claimed my wife owed. I requested formal validation of the debt and asked that it be sent to our current address, which was eventually provided.

Unfortunately, each interaction with this firm has been adversarial and unproductive. The representative I spoke with was dismissive and unwilling to discuss details in a way that would allow me to better understand how or why this debt exists.

The documentation we received consisted of three items, only one of which was itemized, and the other two documents had the account number redacted. The billing codes listed were significantly higher than Medicaid rates and higher than the public self-pay estimates published by the hospital itself.

At the time my wife received care, she may have been eligible for Medicaid or charity care, yet no assistance options were offered by the nonprofit hospital involved. Like many people experiencing a medical crisis, she was required to sign financial responsibility paperwork simply to receive care—without clear information about insurance billing or available financial assistance. Many patients in these circumstances are unaware of assistance programs or lack the resources to advocate for themselves.

This unresolved medical debt has caused severe financial distress for my family. After years of rebuilding our lives and caring for a child with a disability, we are now facing the possibility of bankruptcy over a bill we have repeatedly attempted to validate and understand.

When I contacted the firm again to discuss what appear to be discrepancies in the billing, I was met with the same lack of cooperation. I was told that the invoice reflecting the higher amount was a “settlement figure,” which differed from what I had been told previously. There was no willingness to review the charges, explain additional fees, or discuss resolution. Instead, the conversation focused on the statute of limitations and references to potential legal action, which felt intimidating given our financial circumstances.

Based on my experience, this firm’s approach lacks transparency and places significant strain on individuals who are uninsured or financially vulnerable. South East Medical’s billing practices and its use of third-party debt collection warrant public scrutiny. I would strongly encourage anyone dealing with this firm to carefully document all communications and fully understand their rights before engaging.

After the firm was retained and paid a substantial fee, the level of effort and thoroughness did not align with what had been represented. Certain filings appeared incomplete, and communication was frequently difficult, unproductive, or at times unavailable altogether.

I rarely leave negative reviews, but I feel it’s important to share my experience so that future clients are aware of the unprofessional conduct I encountered with this law firm.
I recently purchased a home situated on one acre of land from a group of four individuals who had inherited an estate from their parents. The full estate consisted of approximately 250 acres, while my purchase represented only a small portion of that property. The most recent recorded deed was the estate deed, which transferred the entire 250 acres from the deceased parents to their children. I also had access to a prior recorded deed that specifically described only my one-acre property.
From the beginning, I made it clear to the firm that I needed the deed preparation handled promptly. After three weeks—with no completed deed and payment already rendered—attorney BriAnne presented a Quitclaim Deed (without consulting me about the type of deed I preferred) that incorrectly described the entire 250-acre estate rather than the one-acre property I purchased. When I pointed out this error and provided her with the prior recorded deed from Houston County Probate Court showing the correct legal description, she refused to use it.
Instead, she insisted that I either pay up to $1,200 for a new survey or proceed with filing the incorrect deed containing the full 250-acre description. Despite being fully aware that the legal description was inaccurate, she still offered to record it as-is, which I found highly concerning and unprofessional.
Although BriAnne ultimately refunded my payment, she did so by leaving the refund check under the doormat at her office—an unorthodox and unprofessional method of returning client funds.
I subsequently contacted another attorney, who immediately recognized the issue, confirmed that a new survey was unnecessary, and agreed to prepare the correct deed using the previously recorded legal description. This second attorney expressed genuine disbelief at the situation I had experienced with BriAnne and her handling of the matter.
I’m unsure whether this issue stemmed from inexperience or an attempt to avoid acknowledging an error, but either way, the fact that she was willing to file a deed with a knowingly incorrect legal description demonstrates a concerning lack of professionalism by this firm.

Just because you don't get paid unless you win.  It doesn't mean you ignore and sabotage certain VA disabled veterans claims with social security. After the judge denied my claim I was gaslighted by your firm. I'm an 100% VA disabled veteran for 5 years now. Your attorney didn't use any evidence to help support my claim. Thanks for withdrawing yourself from my claim.  I feel discrimination was a big factor with my case and it's disgusting what was done to America's veterans.
reviews.ownerResponse:
Because this is a duplicate version of the same 1-star review you previously posted at one of our other office locations on Google (which violates Google's review policies), we are re-posting our original response for clarity and consistency:

We take accusations of this nature very seriously, and we respectfully disagree with your characterization of our firm’s conduct.

Because your review does not include identifying information that allows us to confirm you were ever a client of our firm, we are unable to address the specifics of your claim. That said, the allegation that our firm would intentionally “sabotage” a disability case is simply inconsistent with both our business model and our professional ethics.

Brock & Stout represents Social Security disability claimants on a contingency-fee basis. We are not paid unless benefits are awarded. Quite simply, there is no financial, professional, or ethical incentive for our firm to want a client’s claim to fail. Our success is directly tied to the success of the people we represent.

For nearly three decades, our firm has focused on providing diligent, knowledgeable representation to injured and disabled individuals, including veterans. Our attorneys and staff take pride in advocating for clients at every stage of the disability process and in keeping clients informed about the challenges and realities of these cases, which can be lengthy and complex.

We have the utmost respect for those who have served our country. Any suggestion that we would intentionally work against a veteran’s interests is particularly troubling and does not reflect who we are or how we practice law.

We encourage any former client with concerns about their case to contact our office directly so we can review the matter and address those concerns appropriately. We remain committed to professionalism, transparency, and doing everything ethically and legally possible to pursue benefits on behalf of our clients.

Brock & Stout Attorneys at Law
